questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following properties completely describes a scalar quantity?
questions.hint: Think of quantities like mass or temperature.
questions.explanation: Scalars are defined by magnitude alone, such as mass \(10\text{ kg}\) or temperature \(27^\circ\text{C}\). Option C describes vectors, not scalars.

source_trace.content_anchor: quantities that are fully described by a magnitude alone

explanations_tiered.definition: A scalar is a quantity that has magnitude (numerical value) but no direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Scalars are fundamental in physics and mathematics for describing size or amount without orientation in space.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine your body temperature \(36.5^\circ\text{C}\) — it's just a number. No arrow needed. That's a scalar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A scalar requires only a number and a unit, like \(45\text{ km/hr}\) for speed. Direction is irrelevant for scalar classification.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalars are rank-0 tensors, invariant under coordinate rotation. Their value depends only on the chosen unit system, not spatial orientation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that scalars have no directional component.
Identify which option omits direction completely.
Select 'magnitude only' as the defining property.
misconceptions: Some believe scalars have direction because velocity (a vector) includes speed. Speed is scalar; velocity adds direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scalar: magnitude only. Vector: magnitude + direction.

glossary_entry.definition: A physical quantity described completely by a numerical value (magnitude) and a unit, without any directional information.

flashcard.front: What property alone defines a scalar quantity?
flashcard.back: Magnitude only. For example, \(10\text{ kg}\) of mass is a scalar.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the defining characteristic of a scalar?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A scalar has magnitude only. Examples include mass, temperature, and speed.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quantity fully described by a magnitude (numerical value) alone, without direction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Scalars: magnitude only. Vectors: magnitude + direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: Students think speed is a vector. Emphasise that speed is scalar; velocity adds direction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between scalar and vector quantities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. A scalar has magnitude only; direction is irrelevant to scalar quantities.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A vector quantity requires which two pieces of information for complete description?
questions.explanation: Vectors are fully described by both magnitude (numerical value) and direction, as in \(30\text{ km/hr}\) northeast. Options A, B, and D describe scalar or unrelated pairs.

source_trace.content_anchor: quantities that are fully described by both a magnitude and a direction

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude (size) and direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors are essential for describing physical quantities like force, velocity, and displacement, where orientation matters.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of an arrow: its length is magnitude, where it points is direction. Both matter for a vector.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A vector requires a numerical value with units plus a direction, such as \(10\text{ N}\) upward. Magnitude alone describes a scalar.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vectors are elements of a vector space, possessing both norm (magnitude) and direction. The direction is often expressed via unit vectors: \(\vec{v} = |\vec{v}|\hat{v}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that vectors require both numerical size and orientation.
Identify the option that explicitly names both magnitude and direction.
Eliminate options listing scalar pairs.
misconceptions: Some think velocity and speed are identical. Speed is scalar; velocity includes direction, making it a vector.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vector = magnitude + direction. Scalar = magnitude only.

glossary_entry.definition: A quantity described completely by both a numerical magnitude and a specified direction.

flashcard.front: What two things describe a vector completely?
flashcard.back: Magnitude and direction. Example: \(20\text{ N}\) to the left.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A vector requires which two properties?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Magnitude (size/numerical value) and direction (orientation in space).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quantity fully described by both a magnitude and a direction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Vector: magnitude + direction. Force, velocity, displacement are vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse speed and velocity. Emphasise that direction is the distinguishing feature.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between scalar and vector quantities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. A vector requires both magnitude (numerical value) and direction for complete description.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given the column vector \(\vec{u} = \binom{6}{8}\), what is the magnitude \( |\vec{u}| \)?
questions.explanation: Magnitude is found using \( |\vec{u}| = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36+64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \). Option A gives \(\sqrt{6^2 - 8^2}\) incorrectly; option D multiplies components.

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnitude of a vector is its length, calculated as the square root of the sum of squares of its components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Magnitude represents the size of a vector, independent of its direction, computed via the Pythagorean theorem.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a right triangle with legs 6 and 8. The vector's length is the hypotenuse, which is 10.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Euclidean norm of a 2D vector is derived from the distance formula: \( \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \). This generalises to higher dimensions as \( \sqrt{\sum x_i^2} \).
reasoning_steps: Identify the formula for magnitude of a column vector.
Square each component.
Add the squares.
Take the square root of the sum.

formula_analysis.name: Magnitude (Euclidean norm)
formula_analysis.derivation: From Pythagorean theorem on a right triangle with legs x and y.

misconceptions: Some add components (6+8=14) or multiply (48). The correct operation is squaring, summing, then square root.

glossary_entry.definition: The length of a vector, calculated as the square root of the sum of the squares of its components.

flashcard.front: How do you find the magnitude of \(\binom{3}{4}\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The length of a vector, computed via the Pythagorean theorem.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common mistake: adding components (14). Emphasise squaring first.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the magnitude of a vector given its components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(|\vec{u}| = \sqrt{6^2+8^2} = \sqrt{100}=10\). Option C (14) is sum of components; option D (48) is product.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A vector has components \(x = 4\) and \(y = 4\sqrt{3}\). What is its direction angle \(\theta\) measured from the positive \(x\)-axis?
questions.hint: Use \(\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}\) and recall exact trigonometric values.
questions.explanation: Direction is found using \(\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{4\sqrt{3}}{4} = \sqrt{3}\). \(\tan 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}\), so \(\theta = 60^\circ\). Option A gives \(\tan 30^\circ = 1/\sqrt{3}\), option B gives 1.

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction of a vector is the angle it makes with a reference axis, usually the positive x-axis, calculated using tangent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Direction describes where a vector points. For a vector \(\binom{x}{y}\), \(\tan \theta = y/x\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a right triangle with horizontal side 4 and vertical side \(4\sqrt{3} \approx 6.93\). The steep slope gives about 60 degrees.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}\), we get \(\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{4} = \sqrt{3}\). From trigonometry, \(\tan 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}\), so \(\theta = 60^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Direction angle \(\theta = \arctan(y/x)\) with quadrant consideration. Here both x,y>0 (first quadrant), so \(\theta = \arctan(\sqrt{3}) = 60^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the formula for direction angle.
Divide the vertical component by the horizontal component.
Take the inverse tangent.
Match the result to a known angle.

formula_analysis.name: Direction angle formula
formula_analysis.derivation: From definition of tangent in a right triangle.

misconceptions: Some use \(\tan \theta = x/y\) instead of y/x, giving \(30^\circ\) (option A).

glossary_entry.definition: The angle a vector makes with a reference direction, usually the positive x-axis, found using \(\tan \theta = y/x\).

flashcard.front: A vector has components x=3, y=3√3. What is its direction angle?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The angle a vector makes with the positive x-axis, found using arctan(y/x).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often mix x and y. Emphasise \(\tan \theta = \text{opposite}/\text{adjacent} = y/x\).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the direction of a vector from its components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(\tan \theta = y/x = (4√3)/4 = √3 \Rightarrow \theta = 60°\). Option A (30°) gives \(\tan 30° = 1/√3\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Triangle law of addition of vectors
questions.question: Using the triangle law of vector addition, if \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \vec{u}\) and \(\overrightarrow{BC} = \vec{v}\), what is the resultant vector?
questions.hint: Place the tail of the second at the head of the first; the resultant connects first tail to second head.
questions.explanation: The triangle law states \(\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{BC} = \overrightarrow{AC}\). Vector \(\overrightarrow{AC}\) closes the triangle from A to C. Option A is opposite direction; options C and D are reversed.

source_trace.structural_element: Triangle law of addition of vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Triangle law of vector addition states that if two vectors are represented as two sides of a triangle in order, their sum is the third side closing the triangle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For vectors arranged head-to-tail, the resultant goes from the tail of the first to the head of the last.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Walk from A to B, then B to C. Your total trip is from A to C directly. That's the resultant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Triangle law is a corollary of the parallelogram law: vectors placed head-to-tail form a triangle; the diagonal from first tail to last head is the resultant.
reasoning_steps: Recall the triangle law definition.
Add the two given vectors in order: tail of first at A, head of first at B; tail of second at B, head at C.
The resultant goes from A (first tail) to C (last head).
misconceptions: Some mistakenly choose \(\overrightarrow{CA}\) (the reverse direction). The resultant direction matters; \(\overrightarrow{AC}\) not \(\overrightarrow{CA}\).

glossary_entry.definition: If two vectors are represented by two sides of a triangle taken in order, their sum is the third side taken in the opposite order.

flashcard.back: \(\overrightarrow{AC}\). The resultant goes from A (first tail) to C (second head).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the triangle law give for AB + BC?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: AC. The resultant connects the initial point of the first to the terminal point of the second.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A rule for vector addition where the sum equals the third side of a triangle formed by two vectors placed head-to-tail.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise head-to-tail arrangement to avoid confusion with reversed vectors.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply the triangle law to add two vectors
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Triangle law directly states \(\overrightarrow{AB}+\overrightarrow{BC} = \overrightarrow{AC}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: To subtract vector \(\vec{b}\) from vector \(\vec{a}\) geometrically, which operation is performed instead?
questions.hint: Subtracting a vector is the same as adding its opposite.
questions.explanation: Vector subtraction is defined as \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\), where \(-\vec{b}\) has the same magnitude but opposite direction. Option B correctly states this addition.

explanations_tiered.definition: Vector subtraction is performed by adding the negative of the subtrahend vector to the minuend vector.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Subtraction changes the problem to addition by reversing the direction of the vector being subtracted.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Instead of taking away, you add the opposite arrow: same length, opposite direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: To compute \(\vec{a} - \vec{b}\), we add \(\vec{a}\) and \(-\vec{b}\), where \(-\vec{b}\) is \(\vec{b}\) rotated 180°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Subtraction is not commutative: \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\). The negative vector has the same magnitude but opposite orientation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that subtraction is the inverse of addition.
Express subtraction as addition of a negative.
Identify the negative of \(\vec{b}\) as a vector opposite in direction.
misconceptions: Some think vector subtraction is component-wise magnitude subtraction; it's actually addition of the opposite vector.

glossary_entry.definition: The operation of adding a vector to the negative of another: \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\).

flashcard.front: How is vector subtraction converted to addition?
flashcard.back: Add the negative of the subtrahend: \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you subtract vectors geometrically?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Add the first vector to the negative of the second: \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The process of adding a vector to the opposite of another vector.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that subtracting is not just magnitude difference; direction reversal is critical.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Vector subtraction is defined as addition of the negative: \(\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Addition and subtraction of column vectors
questions.question: Given \(\vec{a} = \binom{5}{-2}\) and \(\vec{b} = \binom{-3}{4}\), find \(\vec{a} - \vec{b}\).
questions.explanation: Column vector subtraction: \(\binom{5}{-2} - \binom{-3}{4} = \binom{5 - (-3)}{-2 - 4} = \binom{8}{-6}\). The correct option is B (duplicate C and D in source are typos; answer is \(\binom{8}{-6}\)).

source_trace.structural_element: Addition and subtraction of column vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Subtraction of column vectors is performed component-wise: subtract horizontal components and vertical components separately.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Subtract top numbers: 5 − (−3) = 8. Subtract bottom numbers: −2 − 4 = −6. Result is (8, −6).
worked_solution: Step 3: Write result as column vector \(\binom{8}{-6}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify both components of each vector.
Subtract the horizontal components.
Subtract the vertical components.
Express the result as a column vector.
misconceptions: Some subtract in the wrong order (r-p instead of p-r), giving \(\binom{-8}{6}\).

glossary_entry.definition: Component-wise subtraction of two column vectors, yielding a new column vector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: subtracting in reverse order. Emphasise first minus second component-wise.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Perform addition and subtraction of column vectors

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
questions.question: If \(\vec{v} = \binom{3}{5}\) and \(k = -2\), what is \(k\vec{v}\)?
questions.hint: Multiply each component by the scalar; a negative scalar reverses direction.
questions.explanation: Scalar multiplication: \(k\vec{v} = \binom{kx}{ky}\). Here \(-2 \times \binom{3}{5} = \binom{-6}{-10}\). Option A has positive components (ignoring sign); C and D mix signs incorrectly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar

explanations_tiered.definition: Scalar multiplication multiplies each component of a vector by a real number, scaling the magnitude and potentially reversing direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When a vector is multiplied by a scalar, each component is scaled by that scalar.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply each number inside the column by -2: 3×(-2) = -6; 5×(-2) = -10. Result is (-6, -10).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For scalar \(k\) and vector \(\binom{x}{y}\), \(k\binom{x}{y} = \binom{kx}{ky}\). Here \(k=-2\), so \(-2\binom{3}{5} = \binom{-6}{-10}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalar multiplication is a linear operation: \(k\vec{v}\) has magnitude \(|k||\vec{v}|\) and direction same as \(\vec{v}\) if \(k>0\), opposite if \(k<0\).
worked_solution: Step 3: Write result as column vector \(\binom{-6}{-10}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the scalar factor.
Multiply each component by the scalar.
Write the resulting column vector.
misconceptions: Some forget to multiply both components, giving \(\binom{-6}{5}\) or similar.

glossary_entry.definition: The operation of multiplying each component of a vector by a scalar, scaling the vector's length and possibly reversing its direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is \(-2(3,5)\) as a column vector?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Multiplying each component of a vector by a scalar.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that a negative scalar reverses direction but the operation is component-wise.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
questions.question: A student claims that multiplying a vector by a scalar only changes its magnitude but never its direction. Is this always true?

options.A: Yes, magnitude always scales but direction remains unchanged
options.B: No, direction reverses when the scalar is negative
options.C: No, direction always reverses for any scalar other than 1
options.D: Yes, direction is independent of scalar multiplication

questions.hint: Consider what happens when the scalar is negative.
questions.explanation: Direction remains the same only for positive scalars. A negative scalar reverses direction (180° rotation). Option B correctly identifies this exception. Options A and D ignore sign; C overstates by saying 'always reverses'.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
source_trace.content_anchor: direction same if k>0, opposite if k<0

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction of a scalar multiple \(k\vec{v}\) is the same as \(\vec{v}\) if \(k > 0\), and opposite if \(k < 0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A negative scalar reverses direction while a positive scalar preserves direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you multiply by -1, the arrow points the opposite way. Multiply by 2, it points the same way but longer.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(k>0\), \(k\vec{v}\) points in the same direction as \(\vec{v}\). For \(k<0\), the direction reverses 180°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Direction of a scalar multiple is determined by sign(k): \(\hat{v} = \frac{\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|}\). Then \(\frac{k\vec{v}}{|k\vec{v}|} = \text{sgn}(k)\hat{v}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of scalar multiplication.
Check effect of positive scalar: direction preserved.
Check effect of negative scalar: direction reversed.
Conclude that direction is not always unchanged.
misconceptions: Believing scalar multiplication never changes direction, ignoring sign of scalar.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scalar sign matters: positive → same direction; negative → opposite direction.

glossary_entry.definition: The direction of \(k\vec{v}\) is the same as \(\vec{v}\) for \(k>0\), opposite for \(k<0\), undefined for \(k=0\).

flashcard.front: When does scalar multiplication reverse a vector's direction?
flashcard.back: When the scalar is negative. Positive scalars preserve direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does scalar multiplication always preserve direction?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No. Positive scalars preserve direction; negative scalars reverse it.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The direction of a vector after scalar multiplication depends on the sign of the scalar.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Positive scalar = same direction. Negative scalar = opposite direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use examples: 2v vs -2v to show direction reversal.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Analyse the effect of scalar multiplication on vector direction
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A negative scalar reverses direction; positive scalars preserve it.
